title: Introduction
subtitle: Love is a complex and diverse emotion that can appear in many different forms. Different people understand and express love in different ways depending on their experiences, relationships, and cultural backgrounds. At UCSB, love is not only limited to romantic relationships. It can also be seen through friendship, emotional support, community care, and academic collaboration. During my field observations, I visited several locations at UCSB, including Carrillo Dining Hall, the UCSB Campus Store, the HSSB Korean Workshop event, and the UCSB Library. While observing students’ interactions, I noticed that many ordinary moments reflected emotional connection and support. Some students expressed love through romantic relationships, while others created belonging and comfort through friendship, shared cultural experiences, and academic teamwork. These observations connect closely to Unit 2 readings by Arthur C. Brooks, Mandy Len Catron, and Emily Esfahani Smith. Their ideas suggest that love is built through trust, vulnerability, emotional safety, and everyday acts of care. Through my observations, I argue that love at UCSB often appears in small daily interactions that help students feel emotionally supported, connected, and socially accepted.

title: Friendship and Daily Love at Carrillo Dining Hall
Cross‑Cultural Community Love at HSSB Korean Workshop
Romantic Collaborative Love at UCSB Campus Store
Conclusion
subtitle: Through my field observations at UCSB, I realized that love exists in many different forms beyond romantic relationships. Throughout campus life, I observed friendship, emotional support, community care, mentorship, and cultural connection in ordinary daily interactions. Although many of these moments appeared simple, they still reflected meaningful emotional relationships that helped students feel supported and connected. The observations showed that love often functions as a source of belonging, motivation, and emotional stability during stressful academic life. Whether students were eating together at Carrillo Dining Hall, participating in cultural activities at the HSSB workshop, studying together in the library, or supporting romantic partners academically, each interaction demonstrated how care and connection strengthen campus relationships. These experiences strongly connect to the Unit 2 readings by Arthur C. Brooks, Mandy Len Catron, Emily Esfahani Smith, and other authors who describe love as trust, vulnerability, kindness, and cooperation. Overall, this project changed my understanding of love at UCSB. I learned that love is not always dramatic or highly visible. Instead, it often appears quietly through small acts of support, attentive listening, shared experiences, and everyday kindness. These ordinary interactions help create a stronger and more emotionally connected campus community.
